title: Emergency Preparedness and Protective Services Committee - May 28, 2026
meeting_location: Champlain Room, 110 Laurier Avenue West, and by electronic participation

agenda_items.title: Notices and meeting information for meeting participants and the public

attachments.attachment_title: Public Notices English - Hybrid Standing Committee 2025 (AODA).pdf
Public Notices French - Hybrid Standing Committee 2025 (AODA).pdf

agenda_items.description:
Notices and meeting information are attached to the agenda and minutes, including: availability of simultaneous interpretation and accessibility accommodations; in camera meeting procedures; information items not subject to discussion; personal information disclaimer for correspondents and public speakers; notices regarding minutes; and remote participation details.
Accessible formats and communication supports are available, upon request.
Except where otherwise indicated, reports requiring Council consideration will be presented to Council on June 10, 2026 in Community and Protective Services Committee Report 24.
The deadline to register by phone to speak, or submit written comments or visual presentations is 4 pm on Wednesday, May 27, 2026, and the deadline to register to speak by email is 8:30 am on Thursday, May 28, 2026.
agenda_items.minutes: These “Summary Minutes” indicate the disposition of items and actions taken at the meeting. This document does not include all of the text that will be included in the full Minutes, such as the record of written and oral submissions. Recorded votes and dissents contained in the Summary Minutes are draft until the full Minutes of the meeting are confirmed by the Committee. The draft of the full Minutes (for confirmation) will be published with the agenda for the next regular Committee meeting and, once confirmed, will replace this document.

agenda_items.title: Declarations of Interest
agenda_items.minutes: No Declarations of Interest were filed.

motions.motion_text:
That, pursuant to Subsection 89(4) of the Procedure By-law (being By-law NO2025-100), the Emergency Preparedness and Protective Services Committee approve that the Rules of Procedure be suspended to allow for the consideration of the item(s) listed as:
Response to Inquiry
4.1 EPPSC 2026-01 – Lithium Battery Fires

Vice-Chair David Hill introduced the following motion to request for provincial action on lithium-ion battery safety.
Mr. Paul Hutt, Fire Chief, Ottawa Fire Services, Emergency and Protective Services Department, spoke to the matter and was present to respond to questions.
The motion was subsequently ‘Carried’, as presented.

sub_agendas_items.title: EPPSC 2026-02 HVAC Noise Emanating from Talisman Apartments at 1354 Carling Avenue
Ottawa Fire Services 2025 Annual Report
sub_agendas_items.minutes:
Mr. Ryan Perrault, General Manager, Emergency and Protective Services (EPS) Department, introduced Paul Hutt, Fire Chief, Ottawa Fire Services, EPS, who prepared the report and was in attendance to respond to questions. Mr. David Andre, Deputy Fire Chief, Ottawa Fire Services, EPS was also in attendance to respond to questions.
Mr. Hutt and Mr. Andre spoke to a detailed slide presentation which served as an overview of the report. A copy of this presentation is held on file with the Office of the City Clerk.
The following member of the public spoke before the Committee and provided comments on the item:
The following Direction to staff was introduced and accepted by staff.
Direction to staff (Councillor C. Kelly)
That staff be directed to provide additional detail in future Annual Reports identifying where historical figures reported in previous Annual Reports have been revised and explaining the reason for those revisions.
Following questions to staff, and at the conclusion of Committee discussions, the report recommendation was ‘Carried’, as presented.

motions.motion_text:
Report Recommendation(s)
That the Emergency Preparedness and Protective Services Committee recommend that Council receive the 2025 Ottawa Fire Services Annual Report for information.

sub_agendas_items.title: Councillor’s Report (Councillor S. Plante) – Strengthening Protections Against Construction Encroachment and Enforcing Committee of Adjustment Conditions
sub_agendas_items.minutes:
At the outset, Councillor Stéphanie Plante introduced her report and spoke to provide context. Councillor Plante then presented a Motion to Amend, with the purpose of amending the original report recommendation.
Mr. Ryan Perrault, General Manager, Emergency and Protective Services (EPS) Department, and Mr. John Buck, Chief Building Official, Building Code Services, Planning, Development & Building Department (PDBS), were present to respond to questions.
The following members of the public spoke before the Committee and provided comments on the item:
Written correspondence was received from the following members of the public and organizations, and is held on file with the Office of the City Clerk:
Following questions to staff, and at the conclusion of Committee discussions, the report recommendation was ‘Carried’, as amended.

agenda_items.title: In Camera Items
agenda_items.minutes: There were no in camera items.

agenda_items.title: Notices of Motions (For Consideration at Subsequent Meeting)

sub_agendas_items.title: Motion – Councillor L. Johnson (on behalf of Councillor T. Kavanagh) – Nuisance Lighting Impacts

motions.motion_text:
WHEREAS the City of Ottawa’s By-law Review Work Plan identifies regulatory by-laws for review or development each Term of Council; and
WHEREAS municipalities have the authority to regulate nuisances including outdoor illumination and indoor lighting that can be seen outdoors pursuant to Section 129 of theMunicipal Act, 2001;and
WHEREAS Property Standards By-law 2013-416 and the Permanent Signs on Private Property By-law 2016-326 set out certain regulations with respect to exterior lighting; and
WHEREAS residents have indicated concerns with light pollution affecting their use and enjoyment of their properties and homes that are not adequately addressed by the existing regulations; and
WHEREAS with the new zoning regulations, which seek to increase urban density, light pollution may become increasingly problematic for both humans and wildlife alike; and
WHEREAS establishing clear standards for outdoor lighting will help mitigate these impacts, promote public health and safety, and protect local ecosystems while still allowing for appropriate illumination in urban spaces; and
WHEREAS other Ontario municipalities including Mississauga and Richmond Hill have by-laws to regulate light pollution.
THEREFORE BE IT RESOLVED that staff include a light pollution by-law, or amendments to the Property Standards By-law concerning light pollution and nuisance, as an item for consideration as part of the development of next Term of Council’s By-law Review Work Planto be approved by Council, and that other jurisdictions be considered as models as part of this work.
